title: Ixora subvelutina
taxonomy: Ixora subvelutina is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. This family is commonly known as the coffee or madder family. The genus Ixora is a group of flowering plants native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world. The Rubiaceae family is one of the largest and most diverse families of flowering plants. Ixora subvelutina is a shrub species that is native to certain parts of Asia.
uses: Ixora subvelutina, a plant species found in Sri Lanka, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. The plant is said to possess antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which are utilized to treat wounds and skin conditions. The leaves and flowers of Ixora subvelutina are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. In addition, the plant is believed to have antibacterial properties, making it effective against infections. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Ixora subvelutina in modern medicine.

intro: Ixora subvelutina, also known as the hairy jungle jessamine, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae, commonly found in tropical regions. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties, which can aid in treating various ailments such as wounds, fever, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Ixora subvelutina include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Ixora subvelutina is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture to harness its potential health benefits.
